title: Web Application Vulnerablities
text: Web Application Vulnerablities Almost every area of a web application, from configuration to data I/O, and from auuthentication to logging needs to be considered when looking at web security. In general you need to be consider the following issues when developing your web application: Identity spoofing—you need to prevent users from acting as if they were any other user, or assuming another user's identity. For more details, see Authentication for Web Applications and Session Management for Web Applications . Data tampering—users can change any data delivered to them and this can change client-side validation, GET data, POST data, cookies, HTTP headers, and so on. The application should not send data to the browser that is obtainable from within the browser itself. In addition, the application should carefully check any data received from the user to identify if it is sane and applicable. For more information, see Data I/O for Web Applications . Elevation of privilege—if an application provides users and administrative roles, it is vital to ensure the users cannot elevate themselves to any higher privilege role, by ensuring all actions must be gated through an authorization matrix to ensure that only the right roles can access privileged functionality. For more information, see Authorization for Web Applications . Information disclosure—the browser cannot be trusted fully to adhere to no-caching policies that can be set by HTTP headers. It is therefore important to minimize the amount of information stored by a browser, just in case it leaks information that could be used by an attacker to learn more about the user or even become that user. For more information, see Browser Security . Denial of service—applications could be abused by a denial-of-service attack, in which requests claim expensive resources, thereby impairing service to other users. Such requests should be reserved for authorized users. See Authentication for Web Applications and Authorization for Web Applications . Repudiation—applications should have repudiation controls, such as web access logs and audit trails at every tier as well as a user context from top to bottom. Ideally the application should run as the user, but within Uniface this is not possible. See Logging and Auditing Web Applications . The following table shows the application areas that need to be considered in a web application, and the types of threats they are vulnerable to. The discussions of the security threats include information on how you can defend against them. Table 1. Vulnerable Application Areas and Their Threats Vulnerable Application Area Threats Authentication for Web Applications Session Fixation Session Sidejacking Cross-Site Scripting Man-In-The-Middle Attacks Identity Spoofing Password Cracking Authorization for Web Applications Access to confidential data Data manipulation Execution of unauthorized commands Privilege escalation Browser Security Eavesdropping Data Leaks Cross-Site Scripting Session Management for Web Applications Session Fixation Session Sidejacking Cross-Site Scripting Data I/O for Web Applications Interpreter Injection Cross-Site Scripting Man-In-The-Middle Attacks Command execution (buffer overflow attacks) Session replay Web Application Configuration and Deployment Eavesdropping Password Cracking Logging and Auditing Web Applications Data Leaks Denial of Service Attack Buffer overflow attacks to gain access to system-level details Was this topic helpful?
